- a general view of the location of the outdoor scenes of crime, in relation to immovable objects viz., electric pole, telegraphic pole, a prominent building, road etc., should be photographed. - the entrance and exit gates of the house should be depicted in the photographs. - the general surroundings like houses, parks, shops etc., if any, near the house should be revealed in the frame of the photo. - in homicide cases, a general picture showing the location of the body, weapon of offence, bloodstains etc., in relation to the room should be reflected in the photograph. - if there is a dead body at the scene of crime the person should be identified in the photograph. - all injuries on the body and the weapon of offence (if found at the scene of crime) should be photographed keeping a scale in the frame. - colour photography is preferred as the items present at the place of occurrence can be depicted in a proper perspective. - all items photographed should be equally focused i.e., depth of focus should be maintained properly while taking general photographs. - as far as possible, normal lens without colour filters should be used while photographing scene of crime. - digital photography and digital video graphy also can be utilized in crime scene recording to impress the court as well as electronically transmitting them for consultation. - however digital photography and video photography are to supplement normal film photography and vhs (video home system) tape recording. this avoids allegations of morphing, alterations etc, and the courts admit such photographs as evidence. - the name of the photographer, date and time of arrival of the photographer, case number, date etc., should be recorded. - the type of camera and lenses/filters used should also be mentioned in the record. - a rough sketch of the scene of crime should be made after photography. - the person drawing the scene of crime sketch should be experienced in the outdoor as well as indoor sketching. - materials like sketching paper, clipboard, colored pencils/pens, steel tape etc., should be taken to the place of occurrence along with another person to help in measuring distances between various objects. - the sketching at the scene of crime is a rough sketch, which need not be drawn to scale but should reflect the exact distances between important objects. - the north south directions of the place of occurrence should be clearly mentioned in the sketch. - if necessary more than one sketch could be drawn, one showing the location with reference to the surroundings and other may show the body in relation to weapon of offence or entrance/exit doors in the room. - any alterations required in the sketch should be made at the place of occurrence taking into consideration the placement of various objects, but no alterations should be made after leaving the scene of crime. - depending on the physical evidence and nature of case either all the three sketches i.e., co-ordinate method, triangulation method and cross projection method (fig1-3) could be utilized or any one of the methods. - a fair sketch has to be prepared by a draftsman to scale from the rough sketch taking precaution to depict north south direction and exact distances between various objects as mentioned in the rough sketch. - rough sketch as well as fair sketch form part of the case documents. - photostat copies of both the sketches should be attached with the police papers. the original sketches should be submitted to the court whenever asked. - the names of the person who prepared the rough sketch and fair sketch, date of preparation, crime number, date etc., should be mentioned on the sketches.

fig.1. coordinate or base line method fig.2. triangulation method fig.3. cross projection method - if fingerprints are clearly seen, they should be photographed keeping a scale (fig.4). latent prints should be developed and photographed (fig.5). - an expert should lift the fingerprint seen/developed by means of a special transparent tape for identification in a laboratory (fig.6). - in case it is not possible to photograph or lift the prints, the article containing the print may be taken to a laboratory for identification. - if the prints are suspected or seen on a glass tumbler, bottle, glass sheet, metallic articles, weapons, etc; they should carefully be packed in strong card board boxes or wooden boxes taking care to see that the object does not move and the prints are not destroyed during transit (fig.7). - apart from details of the case, labels like "this side up" and "caution fragile material" help to a great extent in protecting the exhibit from breakage. - plaster casts of foot impressions are fragile and hence should be handled carefully. - the mould should be sent to the laboratory along with its frame. any attempt to remove the frame may destroy the print. - if only a few moulds are to be sent for comparison, they should be packed separately, sealed and sent. in case more prints are to be sent, they should be packed tightly in a wooden or cardboard box and sealed. care should be taken to see that they do not move during transit. - proper chain of custody should be maintained and specimen seals should be sent along with the parcels to the laboratory. - labeling like "fragile", "do not drop" should clearly be made apart from case details. - sample earth and sample of plaster of paris should be sent as controls. - all bloodstained clothes (also semen and saliva stained clothes) should completely be dried in shade or for short exposure in sun. in rainy season, they may be dried under a fan. otherwise fungus and bacteria disintegrate the blood material in the stains. the dried stained clothes

should be wrapped separately in news papers taking care to see that stains do not contaminate other blood stains on the garment as well as unstained portions. all the clothes belonging to an individual may be made into a single parcel (fig.11). - if liquid blood is present at the scene of occurrence, a clean white cloth (2 sq. inches) may be soaked in it, dried and sent (fig.8). liquid blood/pasty blood stained earth from the scene of crime should not be kept in a metallic tin and sent to the laboratory. they should be dried and packed in a cardboard box and sent to a laboratory. control sample of earth from the adjoining area should be sent to the laboratory. liquid blood from the scene of crime should not be sent as it is. the blood may be soaked on clean cloth, dried and packed in a paper envelope and sent to the laboratory. - if dry clots of blood are present, they may be scraped and kept in small glass vials, labeled and sent (fig.9). - if stains are present on wooden material or on walls, blood may either be scraped or bloodstained portions may be chiseled and sent. - bloodstained weapons like dagger, knife, screwdriver, sickle, etc. should be packed as it is, without the packing material touching the stained area. - the blood stained areas on weapons like knife, lathi, iron rods, etc. should be covered by means of cotton wool/paper (when the stains are dry) and packed by means of cloth and sealed. otherwise, the stained area may be wiped out when the parcel is opened in the laboratory (fig.10). - stained area should not be touched to avoid contamination by body fluids like sweat, epithelial cells from the skin, etc. - the clothes should be packed in cloth bags or paper envelopes but never in airtight containers like plastic boxes, cellophane bags, etc. in fact, no biological material should be packed in airtight containers. - the post-mortem blood sample should be procured and sent wherever possible, labeling outside the container "sample blood of the deceased". - burnt bones are very brittle and so should be handled carefully. otherwise they become small pieces or even powder rendering them useless for determination of sex and estimation of age. - if big pieces of burnt bones are available, they should be packed separately in cotton wool. - all the pieces should be placed in a wooden box or tough cardboard box with sufficient cotton (or) wool packing (fig.12). the parcel should be labeled "fragile" prominently along with case details outside the parcel. - the firearms should not be touched with hands as far as possible otherwise evidence like fingerprints, bloodstains, etc. may get destroyed. the gun should be picked up by the trigger guard or by the corrugated portion of the weapon, while collecting, packing and sealing from the scene of crime (fig.13). - the pellets, cartridge cases and bullets should be lifted by means of gloved hands or by forceps with rubber tubing at both pointed ends. if metallic ends are used directly for lifting, extraneous marks interfere with striations already present resulting in destruction of evidence (fig. 14 & 15). - physical evidence like bullets, pellets, wads, empty cartridge cases, weapons, etc. should always be packed separately, labeled properly and sent (fig.16&17). - if bloodstains or fingerprints are suspected on the weapons, clear indication inviting the attention of the experts in the laboratory, is a must. - normally loaded weapons should not be packed and transported. if unavoidable, it should be mentioned "loaded" in bold letters outside the parcel. - identification marks of the investigating officer (say initial or asterisk mark) are required on the exhibits in the case of firearm cases, as the same may not be substituted at a later stage by any one. this also helps in identifying the exhibits in the laboratory/courts at the time of court proceedings. - identification mark/signature may be made on the weapon near the open end of the barrel on the outer surface or on the back strap of the butt. identification mark on the magazine in case of pistols should be avoided (fig. 18). - in case of empty metallic cartridge cases, identification mark should be made near open ends on the inner surface. in the case of a bullet, the mark should be made on the bottom or near the nose, but never on the side of the bullet. otherwise tell- tale striation marks get destroyed (fig.19 & 20).

- bottles containing liquids should be packed in such a way that fingerprints, if any, on the bottles are not destroyed. after carefully placing the lids, the bottle should be packed in a wooden/card board container in such a way that the bottle does not move during transit (fig.21). - if no prints are suspected on the bottles they should be kept in a cloth bag and sealed. but never seal the cap alone, as the cap can be unscrewed without breaking the seal, and the contents can be replaced and the cap screwed again. to make it tamper proof, sufficient numbers of seals at various places on the cloth bag are necessary (fig.22-24). - in case of phenolphthalein hand wash/pocket wash in trap cases, small rectangular window / slit may be made on the cloth packing to see the colour of the liquid at the time of receipt in the laboratory. this helps the experts to note the color of the liquid in the bottle (fig.25). - approximate quantity of the liquid should be mentioned on the packing along with details of the case. - preferably sealed glass bottles should be kept in a wooden or cardboard container to prevent breakage during transit. fig.4 visible fingerprint with scale fig. 6 lifting of fingerprint on transparent tape fig.5 latent fingerprint development fig. 7 packing of fingerprint on knife fig. 8 collection of blood on cloth fig. 10 packing of blood stained knife fig.12 packing of burnt bones fig. 9 blood clots scraping from floor fig. 11 packing of blood stained clothes fig.13 packing & scaling of small firearm

- manufacturing of illicit liquor is normally carried out in the outskirts of a town or in the fields. the i.o. should look for abandoned buildings and fields with lot of foliage used for hiding clandestine activity of illicit manufacture of liquor. - materials like sugar, gur, carbohydrates; molasses 'kikar' bark, etc. are the ingredients for the manufacture of country liquor. if such materials are found, they should be confiscated and sent for laboratory analysis. - improvised stills like wide mouthed drums, earthen pots are usually used in the manufacture of illicit liquor. they are important physical evidences. - characteristic smell of 'fruity', i.e. distillate, found at the place may be collected and sent for analysis. - the underground storage area of 'fruity' for maturing the drink should be searched for large quantities of illicit liquor. - large earthen stoves (chuhlas) in places other than kitchen and large number of empty bottles, rubber tubes, bladders, etc. are important physical evidence in illicit liquor manufacture cases. - at least 250 ml. of liquor should be collected for chemical examination. - if numerous bottles of illicit liquor are confiscated, a representative random sample, say 15 to 20 samples would be tested in the laboratory and opinion is sent to the investigating agency. - all types of stains, vomit, and faecal matter on the body of the deceased and at place of occurrence are important physical evidences in alcohol poisoning and other poisoning cases - traces of vegetable debris from seeds, leaves, root and stems at the place of occurrence are important and should be collected and sent for chemical analysis. - pestle and mortar and other artifacts used for pounding suspected poisonous materials should be collected carefully and sent for trace chemical analysis. - tablets, powders, liquids etc. found at the place of occurrence in suspected poisoning cases are helpful in identifying the substances. - even empty bottles, labels; aluminum foils etc, from the place of occurrences may be useful in identifying the drug. (fig. 26) - smoking pipes, spoons, syringes etc. found at the scene of crime are physical evidences to prove the usage of prohibited drugs. - some poisons have characteristic smell. the investigating officer should note any suspected smell from the above physical evidence from the place of occurrence. - observation of number of pinprick marks on the forearm of addicts help in the investigation of drug abuse cases. - in cases of acid/alkali poisoning, blisters on the lip, skin of the deceased have evidential value. fig.26 suicide by poison

ascertain the place of burning of the body by observing the smoke pattern as well as the quantity of soot present (fig.27). - find out whether the room in which the body was found (or said to have been found), was bolted from inside or open. normally in suicide cases the room is found bolted from inside. - look for any attempt made by the members of the household to break open the door to save the victim/deceased. - look for water/sand indicating attempt made by the family members to douse the burning victim. - find out if there are any broken doors/window at the place of occurrence suggesting any attempt made to save the victim. - are there any patterns of soot near the door/window crevices if the room is said to have been bolted from inside? - look for soot deposits on the tower bolt inside the room (fig.28-30). - do the soot patterns on the exposed portions of the tower bolt suggest that the door was bolted from inside or otherwise? - find out if any finger print/palm print, impressions of soot disturbance on the walls suggesting any attempt made by the victim to douse the fire. - look for the presence of incendiary material like kerosene, petrol, diesel, etc. in a container at the place of occurrence (fig.31). - is there any smell of incendiary material like kerosene at the place of occurrence? - were there any containers holding inflammable substances? - search for matchbox or a lighter at the place of occurrence and also look for burnt/un-burnt match sticks at the place. - note the position of the dead body and the place where it was found. - find out whether the body was completely burnt or partially burnt. which portion of the body was burnt more? (fig.32 & 33). - find out if there are any broken pieces of bangles. - was there an un-broken piece of bangles on the wrist of the victim? (fig.34). - could you find the hand of the victim in pugilistic position? (fig.35).

- observe whether the household items like sofa, bed, chairs, tables, etc. in the room are affected by fire or not. - has the body been sent for post mortem immediately? - did you ask the doctor to examine the trachea for the presence of soot particles at the time of post mortem? (fig. 36). - did you ask the doctor to collect blood from the victim to find out the presence of carboxyl - hemoglobin? have you sent the sample to forensic laboratory? - what is the colour of the post-mortem blood? - what is the condition of the hyoid bone fractured/broken or otherwise? - could you find any ante mortem injuries on the body? - did you ask the doctor to look for ante mortem injuries on the dead body? - what types of burn injuries was present on the body? are they anteing mortem or post mortem injuries? fig.27 less amount of soot in homicide burning fig. 28-30 soot deposits in open and closed tower bolts fig.31 kerosene containers in bride burning case fig. 33 less burning on the back fig. 35 pugilistic pose due to third degree burns fig. 32 maximum burning on the upper portion of body fig. 34 unbroken glass bangles in dowry death case fig. 36 soot practices in trachea in dowry death case

- all bloodstained patterns at the scene of crime and the patterns on the clothes of the deceased should be photographed (fig.37-39). - individual stains at the scene of crime should be photographed keeping a scale to indicate the angle of assault, size of the stain, pattern of the stain due to height, etc (fig. 40). - if need be, distance between the stain patterns may also be included in the record to indicate primary and secondary scenes of crime. this clearly establishes the place of assault and the place where the body was abandoned after transportation (fig. 41 & 42). - trail of bloodstains and size and shape of the drops of blood may indicate the direction and height at which the body was transported. - the extent of bloodstain pattern may indicate the type of weapon used, i.e. chopper, sword, knife, lathi, etc. - blood patterns on the walls, on the floor, on the bed, etc. may also indicate the part of the body affected by the weapon of offence. - scattering of various types and sizes of bloodstains also may indicate number of persons injured at the place of occurrence. bloodspaterlmpat 90807 50020 fig. 37-39 blood stain patterns at different angles fig. 41 primary soc at place of occurrence - demo fig. 42 secondary sco in car - demo

- ascertain whether the victim is a major or a minor girl. - let the victim narrate the incident without any prompting. - send the victim immediately for medical examination. examine the scene of occurrence especially bed, sofa etc., for any disturbances from its normal state. - collect clothes /bedding from the cot/sofa. - ask the doctor to collect vaginal swabs and swabs from other parts of the body like abdomen, thighs etc., if necessary. the swabs should be dried and packed (fig. 43). - clothes worn by the victim should be dried and packed separately and sent to the laboratory for examination as in the case of blood stained clothes. - during interrogation of the suspect, circumstances leading to rape and whereabouts during the period of incident should be ascertained. - clothing worn by the suspect at the time of committing crime should be collected. it should be checked whether the same is washed or not. - pieces of cloth/rope if used as gag or used for strangulation should be collected. - photographs/sketches should be prepared of the scene of occurrence. fig. 43 dried vaginal swab - look for hairs as physical evidence at the scene of crime in homicide, assault and rape cases. - while collecting deceased clothes at the time of post-mortem, care should be taken to pack the exhibits carefully so that hairs from the deceased/culprit are not lost during packing and transit. - in rape cases, it is essential to collect undergarments like salwar, petticoat, underwear, etc. after medico-legal examination. they should be packed and sent carefully so that pubic hairs if any from victim/culprit are not lost (fig.44). - sample hair from the suspect(s) should be collected by combing but should not be plucked forcibly. - if sample hair cannot be obtained by combing, at least ten hairs may be cut from as near the roots as possible and sent to the laboratory for comparison mentioning the site from where the hairs were collected. - the doctor should collect the hair samples for comparison from the dead body at the time of post-mortem if needed for comparison. - in case of rape, pubic hair from the victim as well as the culprit should be collected by the doctor and sent to the laboratory for comparison. - for comparison of head hair only samples of head hair from different areas from the suspect(s) are to be collected. head hairs cannot be compared with public or body hair. - various types of fibers from the scene of crime can be collected by means of a special vacuum cleaner with filter to recover minute fibers for comparison (fig.45). - in case a few small fibers are suspected at the scene of crime, a transparent cello-tape can be used to lift the fibers and sent to the laboratory for identification. - in poaching cases, animal hairs are collected from different parts of the animal for comparison with those collected from the suspect's clothes. the weapons used in killing the animal may have hairs sticking along with the blood. these hairs should be collected for examination in the laboratory.

fig. 44 collection of public hairs from clothes in a case fig. 45 collection of hairs/fibers in vacuum filter - all bloodstained exhibits including clothing should be thoroughly dried in air and packed carefully in paper envelopes. airtight bottles or plastic bags should be avoided. - each item of the dried stained exhibit should be packed separately taking care that stained areas do not contaminate unstained areas. covering the stained areas with clean paper can do this. - blood soaked soil; hair, grass, etc. should be thoroughly dried in air and kept in paper envelopes. similarly, unstained soil should be collected and sent as control. - dried blood from absorbent surfaces such as floors, furniture, etc. should be removed by scraping or by chipping off the stained areas and kept in separate containers. - material from representative unstained areas should be sent as control. - if a pool of blood is present at the scene of crime, clean cotton cloth should be soaked in it, dried thoroughly and forwarded for analysis. wet cotton swabs and wet soil mixed with blood disintegrate very fast and therefore useless for dna testing. - stained knife, axe, bullet, sticks, etc. should be secured separately in boxes with the stained areas covered by paper after drying. - post-mortem blood kept for a long time at room temperature disintegrates rapidly and is useless for examination. whenever possible, the post-mortem blood should be stored in a refrigerator and should be sent immediately to the laboratory. alternatively, the blood may be taken on a piece of cloth, dried and packed in a sealed paper envelope. - semen stained clothes should be dried and packed in the same manner as blood stained clothes. - semen stained areas should be marked, if possible, by pencil. - vaginal and cervical swabs collected during medical examination should be sent immediately after drying. - in murder, assault and rape cases, hairs on clothes of both the victim and the suspect should be collected 13. collection of control hairs for comparison by combing from the suspect/deceased is preferred. - method of collection employed and sites of collection of hair should be clearly stated in the forwarding letter and samples should be properly labeled.

- saliva can be swabbed from bite marks of the victim. - fresh or wet tissues should be kept in a clean container, chilled in ice or in a refrigerator and sent to the laboratory at the earliest opportunity. - if delay is unavoidable before dispatching the tissue, it should be divided into two parts. one part is preserved in normal saline and the other is desiccated (dried completely). - bones taken from under the mud should be sent as such packed carefully without fracturing. - stains, tissues, etc. should not be touched with fingers as the sweat, skin cells and dirt may contaminate the stains. - separate items of clothing must be packed and labeled separately. - airtight containers should not be used for stained exhibits. - all relevant information like source and type of specimen, date of collection etc., must accompany each and every sample. other possibilities like contamination of sample with other organisms, tissue, dna, etc. must also be recorded clearly. - sometimes, the quantity of forensic specimen is small and limited and cannot be obtained second time. these materials (exclusive of blood, semen swabs, etc.) should be stored at low temperatures. - only an authorized person should collect the sample. throughout the process of collection, storage, handling and delivery of the sample to the laboratory, the integrity of the sample should be ensured. that means the chain of custody has to be maintained. - blood and saliva stains - at the scene of crime. - fingerprints - all types i.e., visible and latent prints for identification. - footprints - foot impressions in the soil and on the floors for finding the path taken by the culprits as well as for comparison purposes (fig.4648). - firearms and ammunition - for identification and comparison. - explosives - identification from traces and its relevance to the crime. - weapons - different types of weapons like lathi, knife, sword, etc. used in the crime (fig.49). - chemical residues-firearm/explosive residues at the scene of occurrence/on the body of the deceased. - tools - different tools used by the culprits like saw, chisel, crow bar, etc. - tool marks - tool marks present at the site for comparison (fig.50-52). - fibers and hairs - transfer of this material to establish link between the perpetrator and the scene. - glass, dust and paint - to link the suspect with the crime. - check number plates/erased numbers on vehicles - vehicles used by the culprit for committing crimes (fig. 53 & 54). - tire impression of vehicles at the scene of crime if any to link the culprits with the crime (fig. 55 & 56). - splinters of wood and vegetable matter- helps in linking the place of occurrence with the culprit. - ropes, strings, gag, etc. - used by the criminals at the place of occurrence. - adhesive tapes used to gag victims. - list of valuables like jewelers, electronic equipments, etc. - to verify from pawnshops, jewelers and neighbors for identification. - past history of the suspect to verify the person or gang operating in a particular area to trace the culprits.

- cameras with wide-angle lens along with sufficient number of films/digital camera / video camera. - search light. - powerful torch light. - fingerprint kit. - spot kit for identification of blood. - spot kit for identification of explosive residues. - drawing equipment including lead and colour pencils. - chalk-white as well as colour. - firearm residue identification kit. - plaster of paris for lifting footprints in soil. - footprint lifters for lifting foot prints on floors. - pieces of clean cloth/thread for lifting liquid blood. - scalpel / knife to lift dried blood. - different types of packing materials like wooden boxes, cardboard boxes etc. - packing cloth. - needle and string for packing. - seal with lace for sealing the parcels. - paper, pencil and pen for making notes. - measuring tape for finding distances between the objects. - various sizes of envelops for packing and forwarding purpose. - plasticize, m-seal, etc. for lifting tool marks. - metallic / wooden frames for lifting foot/shoe prints from the soil. - shellac with pump and oil/talcum powder to lift foot moulds. - magnifying lens. - scissors, forceps, spoons, etc. - toolbox to open doors, boxes, almirahs etc. - adhesive and transparent tape. - surgical gloves. - compass. - number cards - as soon as they reach the scene of crime, the investigating officers should visually inspect the locks, door handles, key- whole areas, etc. to see whether any force applied in opening the premises or the almirahs. - all vulnerable areas should be checked for fingerprints, bloodstains, fibers, etc. before handling locks as well as other affected artifacts. - as far as possible, all the items with the tool marks should be photographed. - if possible, the items bearing tool marks should be carefully packed and sent to the laboratory for detailed examination. - if tools used in tampering the locks are found at the scene of crime, they should also be packed and sent. - in wire theft cases, the cut ends should not be touched by hand or should not be handled roughly. the cut ends should be covered by means of sponge or cotton wool and the end is secured by means of a plastic bag to prevent from any further scratches (fig.57). - if the cut mark and tool mark items cannot be transported to a laboratory, their casts may be taken at the site by means of plasticize, dental cast material or plaster of paris (if the impression is large in size) and sent for comparison. - the best method however, is to take the impression using m-seal as it takes the marks when it is soft and becomes hard when it is dried. this helps in giving a permanent tool mark for future identification with the suspected tool (fig.58). - care should be taken not to destroy the tool marks if casts are made by means of plasticize, dental cast material and plaster of paris, as they are soft and brittle. they should be secured in cardboard/wooden boxes and kept along with sufficient cotton wool. - it is very essential to keep a photographer ready to take photographs while restoration of numbers work is carried out on motor vehicles and on metal surfaces by conventional methods, otherwise the numbers may appear for a short time and vanish quickly, due to chemical action of acid on metal.

fig. 57 pliers used for cutting wire for comparison fig. 58 impression of screw driver used in burglary on m-seal - as soon as the information is received about the accident, immediate action should be taken to look for the offending vehicle. the police enroute should be alerted. - the place of occurrence should be photographed in such a way that a clear idea regarding the direction of the vehicle is known. if there is death due to accident, clear photograph of a dead body is a must. - a complete sketch of the accident area is required. - physical evidence like paint flakes for spectrographic analysis in the laboratory, splinters of wood from the body of the vehicles, headlight and tail light portions; oil or grease marks on the vehicle, etc. should be photographed, noted and collected (fig.59). - tire impressions on the berg and skid marks on the road should be photographed (fig.60). - tire impressions on soil may be lifted by means of plaster of paris. - all motor workshops en route should be searched for any damaged vehicle for repair. - if a suspected vehicle is found it should be checked for recent repainting and any change in the number plate. - the suspected vehicle should be used for testing skid marks at various speeds for comparison with the skid marks at the place of occurrence to determine the speed of the offending vehicle at the time of accident. - bloodstains, tissue material, cloth pieces, fibers, hairs, etc., if present on the suspected vehicles, should be collected and sent for examination. - if there is death due to accident on the road, the clothes of the victim should be thoroughly checked for dust prints of tires, grease marks, mud, sand, etc. and sent to the laboratory for detailed examination (fig.61 - 63). - in some cases, we may find tire impression on skin of the deceased. careful examination of the dead body may give valuable clues (fig.64).

fig. 59 emission spectrograph for paint analysis fig. 61 dust prints on shirt in accident case fig. 63 dust prints caused by the tire in the accident fig. 60 skid marks of vehicle in accident fig. 62 dust prints on shirt under uv light fig. 64 tire impression on skin in accident case - if wet soil or sand if present on shoes, clothes, vehicles, etc; they should be dried and packed. - samples from different areas on the above mentioned articles should be packed separately. - if oil, grease or any other sticky material is found to adhere to clothes, shoes, etc., leak proof containers like plastic bags should be used for packing. - if dry loose soil, dust, glass, etc. are found, they should be packed in plastic or glass vials. avoid using envelopes for packing these exhibits. - packing of physical evidence should be so thorough that further leakage, breakage, loss of the sample, etc., should not take place during transit. - control soil sample from the adjoining area near the place of occurrence should be collected and sent along with test sample to the laboratory to carryout density gradient analysis of comparison of soils (fig.65). fig. 65 comparison of soils by density gradient analysis

- paint flakes present at the scene of accident should be collected carefully so as not to break they further into small pieces. this helps in physical matching with the paint on the suspected vehicle. - the paint flakes should be kept in a small pillbox, tightly closed, sealed and sent. - in hit and run accident cases, look for paint flakes at the scene of crime on the offending vehicle (when caught) and vehicle of the victim. all three samples are important for spectrographic comparison in the laboratory. - in burglary cases, during breaking open of window/door, paint from the wooden portion may be mixed with wood meal, traces of wood meal may be present on the tools used in such cases. even these minute traces of paint can be analyzed and compared with the paint on the window / door. - if the tool used in the burglary is caught subsequently, the same may be packed carefully so that traces of paint in the crevices of the tool may be recovered and used for comparison in the laboratory. - in accident cases, hit and run cases, mob violence, etc., glass pieces are frequently encountered. the pieces should be collected carefully and sent to the laboratory for comparison. - in case of gunshot fire on windowpane, car windshield, etc, characteristic patterns of fracture of glass are made. examination of the glass in such cases from the hole and the fracture helps in finding the direction of fire. such glasses/ vehicle should be sent to the laboratory for examination (fig.66&67). fig. 66 entrance holes on car windshield fig. 67 exit holes on car windshield - identification of firearms and ammunition. - identification of the shooter from gunpowder residues on hands and clothes (fig. 68). - elemental composition of gun shot residue (gsr) by sem-edxa (fig. 69). - determination of time of firing (old or recent). - determination of direction of firing. - number of rounds fired. - determination of range (distance) of fire from revolvers and smooth bore firearms fig. 70-76). - types of injuries due to firearms i.e. entry and exit wounds (fig.77-80). - injuries caused due to ricochet. - reconstruction of events/situations in firing cases from scene of crime. effectiveness of firearms by finding out velocity of the ammunition in test firing (fig.81). - identification of firearm to determine whether the weapon is company made or country made by testing the velocity in the laboratory. - determining whether the bullet / cartridge were fired from the suspected weapon or otherwise. - determining whether the death was due to homicide, suicide or accident at the scene of crime.

fig. 70 experiment on dummy to find range of firing fig. 71-73 range of firing on dummy by smooth bore firearm fig. 74-76 range of firing on dummy from rifled firearm fig. 77 & 78 entry and exit wounds due to smooth bore firearm fig. 79 & 80 entry and exit wounds due to country made firearm fig. 81 determination of velocity of a firearm by photoelectric cells

- one path for both entry and exit should be demarcated for entering of the authorized personnel. - unauthorized or non-essential should not be allowed into the site. - only one person must assume control of the scene and investigation. - scene of crime boundaries should be established. normally the apparent scattered debris plus 20% additional distance may be considered as the limit. - before physical search is commenced, the place should be declared safe by the explosive expert (anti-sabotage team). - structural integrity (if the explosion had occurred in a building) and safety hazards (eg: gas leaks) must be assessed and dealt with by the qualified experts. - witnesses must be identified and questioned before they disappear regarding the type of smoke, smell at the site and movement of the suspected persons near the site of explosion. - the search should be carried out by a team comprising of a police officer, explosives disposal expert and a forensic specialist. - the explosive disposal expert should check for booby traps. - photographs of the site should be taken and a sketch is prepared. - chemicals, apparatus, notebooks, literature etc., at the site should be examined by forensic expert. - initial opinions by the scientist and explosive expert should be passed on to the investigators. - the physical evidence should be secured by a responsible police officer. - collection of trace materials of explosive substances by swabbing the palm, backside of the hand, in between the fingers and nail crevices on cotton wool are required for analysis in the laboratory. - power sources: vary from car batteries to small button type batteries. - circuitry: fragments of printed circuit boards and wires (fig. 82 & 83). - timers: electronic, mechanical and chemical in ieds (fig.84). - switches: pressure mats, clocks, timers, electronic relays. - detonators and igniters: parts of detonators, sometimes along with wires, safety fuse etc. - explosive charges: commercially manufactured or homemade, can often be identified from fragment of wrapper or residues on ied components or adjacent items. - adhesive tapes: adhesive tapes are generally used in the manufacture of an ied; they usually survive explosion. - ied containers: metal tins, metal pipes, cardboard boxes, suitcases, transistor radios, cloth bags etc.,(fig.85). - bomb making equipment: roles of tape, wires etc., chemicals etc. - the area should be cleared of the curious onlookers by posting guards at a sufficiently distant place. - the area near the device should be cordoned off preferably with sand bags, but the top portion should not be covered in the case of live explosive. - smoking, lighting matches etc, should be prohibited in the area. - requisition for the help of inspector of explosives or a trained explosive expert from fsl/cfsl should be sent immediately. - only one person experienced in explosives should handle the device after wearing protective shields or jackets for dismantling the device. the shield/jacket should be flame, heat and splinter proof. - in case the device is suspected to be highly dangerous, the same may be destroyed by means of bomb disruptor (fig.86). - in case no immediate help is received from explosive expert, the device may be left for at least 24hrs with full police surveillance. - the device may be viewed by binoculars or using a telephoto lens in order to identify labels, special structural features, identification of fuses, initiating devices etc. - the site near the device should be checked for the presence of foot prints and hand bills or any other physical evidence. - in the post blast cases, all suspected physical evidence like powders, burnt fragments, metallic pieces, jute strings, fuse wires etc; should be

- carefully collected and sent to the laboratory for examination in low & high explosives (fig.87 & 88). - it is advised to contact an expert from forensic science laboratory to identify and collect the evidence from the scene of occurrence like vehicles, premises etc. he may also help in reconstructing the scene and the chain of events led to the situation fig (89 & 90). fig. 82 & 83 circuit boards in ieds fig. 84 diagram showing time delay mechanism from watch in ieds fig.85 ied in grey 7 red bags in an explosion case fig. 87 country made bomb with jute string cover fig. 89 damage to a bus due to high explosive fig. 86 explosive disruptor fig. 88 remnants of country made bomb after explosion fig. 90 damage in a train due to high explosive

- determine the type of arson, i.e. due to burning or smoldering (fig.91 & 92). - look for the area of maximum damage to find out the origin of fire. - look for physical evidence like inflammable materials and their containers. - look for matchbox and burnt matchsticks and beedi /cigarette stubs. - check electrical installations like meters, switches, switchboards, junction boxes, etc., for blackening, melting of wires indicating short-circuiting (fig.93-95). - check for loose wires and exposed electrical wires. - check the insurance records of the owner of the place. - examine entrance and exit areas to see whether the place is accessible to outsiders for committing acts of sabotage. - collect burnt wires; fuse boxes, meters, etc. for examination in the laboratory. - before the material is collected, the entire area, especially the electrical. installations should be photographed. - incendiary materials like petroleum products, containers, burnt/semiburnt debris and any other suspicious materials should be sent to the laboratory for examination. - in case of difficult circumstances ask the help of forensic experts. fig. 91 arson due to smoldering in a godown fig. 92 arson due to fire in school function fig. 93 blackening due to high voltage fig. 94 blackening on copper wires due to high voltage fig. 95 melting and bead formation on copper wires due to high voltage

- comparison of signatures/writings of the questioned documents, with the help of admitted signatures/writings, is to ascertain genuine or forged documents (fig.96- 99). - deciphering erased, obliterated and altered writings (fig.100 -103). - identification of type scripts i.e., to find make of the typewriter and then identifying a particular machine (class characteristics & individual characteristics) (fig. 104 & 105). - deciphering indented writings i.e., impression of writings on paper due to pen/pencil pressure by electrostatic document analyzer (esda) (fig. 106 - 108). - detection of forgeries in lottery tickets and currency notes (fig. 109 112). - analysis of paper used in document examination including age of ink. - examination of seals and stamps of postal origin with video spectral comparator (vsc) (fig. 113 - 115). - examination of perforations around stamps, receipts, cheques etc. (fig.116). - reconstruction of charred, mutilated documents. - if the disputed writing is limited and if the signature is only an initial it may not be possible for the expert to give clear-cut opinion. - to pin-point whether a particular forged signature is made by a particular person may not be possible. the only opinion possible is whether the signature/writing is forged or not. - where the specimen signatures/admitted signatures, similarly specimen writings/admitted writings are not sufficient for comparison, no definite opinion could be expected from an expert. - mutilated documents may not give satisfactory results. - photocopies are not always useful to form definite opinion as they sometimes have partial reproduction of the written material. - the determination of the age of the document and age of ink cannot be ascertained to a particular month and year. only approximate estimation of age could be given. - specimen signatures/writings should be taken when the suspect is relaxed on a comfortable chair with a table for writing. - the pen, ink and paper used for the specimen writings should be as nearly similar to those of the questioned documents. - the questioned document should not be shown to the suspect and also he should not be tutored to write in a particular way. - the text of the specimen writing should be the same as that of the questioned writing in the same language as similar words/letters are written by the suspect to help in comparison. at least the written matter may be of one page (say 10 sentences) for meaningful comparison between the two documents. - in order to find out whether the suspect is deliberately trying to disguise writing, at least three dictations of the text writing should be done. - as far as signatures are concerned, at least 20 to 30 signatures may be taken on sample receipts. it is not possible for the suspect in spite of his best efforts to disguise the signatures in such a large sample. - do not mark unnecessarily on the documents with a pen or a pencil unless it is absolutely necessary. - mark q1, q2, q3, etc., about the questioned portion of the writings/signatures taking care to see that the disputed portion of the document is not affected. - no tick marks or cross marks should be made to invite attention of the expert. - do not fold nor roll the document to be examined. - preferably keep the documents in a file but never get them laminated for safety. the lamination may tear the document while opening or destroy the document by adhering to the plastic sheet. - do not remove any portion of the document. - whenever asked, produce specimen signatures/writings and admitted signatures/ writings immediately.

- a close rapport with a document expert is essential for the police officer in questioned documents examination. fig. 96 questioned document in hindi fig. 98 genuine signature fig. 100 signature chemically erased fig. 97 standard document for comparison fig. 99 forged signature fig. 101 signature restored by vsc fig. 102 obliteration of signature fig. 104 questioned typed document fig. 106 indented writing fig. 103 restoration of signature by vsc fig.105 standard typed document for comparison fig. 107 esda machine fig. 108 deciphered by esda

fig. 109 forged lottery ticket fig. 111 fibers & security thread under uv fig. 110 arrows showing forged numbers fig. 112 latent denomination on genuine note fig. 113 video spectral comparator (vsc) fig. 114 postal emblem with cancelation fig. 115 cancelation stamp under vsc fig. 116 matching of perforations in a cheque

- inspection of scene of crime (soc) in cyber crimes should be carried out in the presence of technically qualified independent witnesses. - potential soc is not just the computer alone. the work area, office, cubicle is a soc. - check whether the computer is networked. - do not seize any computer/computer peripherals without the advice of a computer expert. - isolate the suspect working on the computer and interrogate him/her. during interrogation ensure that the suspect does not touch the computer. - photograph/video graph and sketch the soc with proper labeling including cables, connections and external devices attached or unattached to the computer. - do not perform any key board strokes or mouse clicks. - do not perform any examination of the computer or computer device as it may alter the evidence. - collect fingerprints from the computer/computer peripherals if required. - search for external memory devices like pen drive, hard disc etc. - collect ram dump and system information, encrypted files if any by the io (if he has adequate computer knowledge) or take the help of a cyber forensic expert. - photograph, record and note the screen contents. - do not shut down the computer. pull out the power lead directly from the computer not from the wall point. - open the cpu and take photograph of inside view showing all peripherals like hard disk, ram, motherboard etc. - remove the hard disc. - photograph the hard disc showing sl.no of the disc, connector points, jumper position and logic board. - create 2 more images of the original hard disc and other necessary devices seized with 'writer blocker' (device to acquire information on a drive and protect the device contents). - calculate hash value. - prepare a seizure memo listing unique s.no. of external drives, hard disc and hash value of hard disc and other external devices. - packing and forwarding of hard disc: the hard disc has fragile magnetic media. hence they should not be subjected to shocks. they may be packed in anti-static packing or thermocol packing and taped properly to prevent from magnetic devices, temperature and humidity. - first image to be sent to the fsl/cfsl along with brief history of the case, seizure memo and questionnaire. as per the procedure, the case should be sent with the permission of the court. - the second image to be kept with the io as a reserve sample well packed and sealed. - the third image to be handed over to the suspect. - original hard disc and other external memory devices along with seizure memo to be sent to the court along with other original documents at the time of submission of final report/ at the time of trial. - most of the procedures mentioned above are applicable when the computer is in 'power off condition.

- threatening telephone calls in kidnapping cases and calls for harassment of family members can be tapped by the investigating officers with the help of telephone department, only with the approval of the competent authority. - the time and place of such calls can be traced and tape-recorded for evidence with the help of telephone department. - when the calls are traced from a particular telephone, the suspect(s) should be asked to telephone from the same instrument a similar message; and the control sample voice should be recorded in the same tape recorder for comparison by voice spectrograph (fig.117-120). - the report from the expert is an important tool to elicit confession from the perpetrator of the crime. fig. 117 voice spectrograph machine fig. 119 matching of voice the suspect fig. 118 voice spectrograph of a suspect fig. 120 voice spectrograph of innocent - first search the vulnerable places for fingerprint evidence in various types of cases in bright light by means of a magnifying lens. - if the prints are clear, take photographs with a scale in the frame. then lift the same by means of transparent tape for comparison. - if the print is not clear, or if it is partial, it should be developed using appropriate finger print powder. cover the area by tapping the powder using a camel hairbrush. then remove excess powder my means of ostrich hairbrush moving it along the direction of ridges (121). - graphite powder is used for latent fingerprints on glass, white paper, white painted surface etc. - grey powder is used for latent prints on metal surfaces, colored enamel, dark colored paper and colored paint surface. however care should be taken to see the contrast of the background while using appropriate powder. - anthracites powder is used for latent print development on multicolored surfaces and viewed under uv light (fig.122-124). - magnetic powder along with magna brush is used for suspected print on paper/book etc. (fig.125). - while developing the print, pressure should not be applied. otherwise the prints may get wiped out. - it should be ensured that the powder is dry and no mounds of powder are present. - the developed print should be photographed keeping a scale. - the same should be lifted by means of a transparent tape for future comparison. - if the latent prints cannot be developed due to any reason at the place of occurrence, the article may be packed in such a way that the prints are not destroyed during transit to a finger print laboratory. - elimination prints from members of the house should be collected to identify fingerprints of the suspect (s).

fig. 121 removal express powder by fig. 123 developing prints with fluorescent fig. 122 fingerprints on multi colored fig. 124 fingerprints on multi colored fig. 125 developing fingerprints on paper by magna brush - visit to the scene of occurrence by the police and forensic scientists is helpful to understand the cause of death. - external appearance of face, eyes, tongue, cars, etc. should be noted. patechial hemorrhages, froth near mouth/nose if any, should be recorded. - struggle marks if any, physical evidence in nails and other body parts are important to be noted. - position of the neck, its injuries, ligatures, knots on the ropes/strings, appearance, position, dimension and number of ligature marks in case of death due to hanging and throttling should be noted (fig. 1-3). clenched fist should be checked in case of drowning cases for weeds, sand and mud. - external injuries on the body, especially abrasions on legs and head injuries should be noted in case of strangulation, smothering etc. - appearance of skin, especially corrugated appearance on feet and palms, froth from mouth and nose if present, should be noted at the place of occurrence in case of drowning cases (fig.4&5). - fluid from lungs, if any, may be got collected at the time of post-mortem for detection of algae and mud particles in drowning cases (fig.6 & 7). - photograph of the scene of occurrence is a must. complete photograph of the deceased with required enlargements are essential. fig. 1 suicide due to partial hanging fig. 2 ligature mark in suicide

fig. 3 ligature mark due to fig. 4 froth from the mouth in drowning fig. 5 corrugated foot due to drowning fig. 6 pinnate diatom from fresh water fig. 7 centric diatom from marine - all suspected vials with or without liquid/powders should be collected and sent for examination. - any traces of vegetable materials suspected to be from poisonous plants including leaves, seeds, flowers, stems etc. from the scene of occurrence should be sent for identification (fig.8-10). - any items used for pounding to make powder, like 'silbatta', anvil and other items should be checked for vegetable trace material. they should be packed and sent for detailed examination in the laboratory. - materials like vomit, saliva, any other liquid material from the place as well as from the body should be collected for detailed examination in the laboratory. - in case of acid or alkali poisoning corrosion of the skin could be seen mostly around the mouth. - characteristic smell of various poisons viz, malathion, parathion, aldrex, sulphos, arsenic etc. near the mouth of the deceased indicates the type of poison taken/ ingested. - in case of death due to methyl alcohol, characteristic smell of alcohol could be felt at the scene of crime as well as on the body of the deceased. - in case of death due to drugs of abuse, injection marks on the forearm and other body parts could be observed on the dead body. the trace materials of the drugs used for injecting/inhaling could be found at the place of occurrence (fig.11-23). - in suspected death due to poisoning cases, inquest has to be done. - postmortem examination by a forensic pathologist is a must to establish the cause of death. - viscera collected at the postmortem should be sent fsl/cfsl to determine the poison in the viscera. fig. 8 datura plant fig. 9 calotropis plant

- in starvation deaths, skin looks dry, i.e. parchment like, with bony prominences sunken cheeks, dry and cracked lips, feeble pulse, low b.p., and oedema of the feet are common features (fig.24). - exposure to high temperature for a long period of time results in heat exhaustion, and heat stroke. - heat cramps are normally found in the people working in foundries, firemen, etc. who perspire profusely. - heat exhaustion or heat syncope is associated with people who are exposed to long time in hot and humid climate. they are identified with profuse perspiration, vomiting, sub-normal temperature and muscular weakness resulting in circulatory failure. - heat stroke or sunstroke results from exposure to excessive heat beyond 43 degree celsius and above for a long period. respiration in such people is quick resulting in gasping. - exposure to cold for long time results in low temperature of the body, which is called hypothermia. this is also seen in the people suffering from diseases, injuries, drug addicts, those who are deprived of food and people intoxicated due to alcohol. fig. 24 death due to starvation - find the height of the building/structure, if death was suspected due fall from height. - find the distance between the body and the basement at the ground level. - examine bloodstain patterns on the floor/on the ground. - look for stains from the walls or any intermediary construction on the clothes/on the body of the deceased to ascertain whether death was due to accident or otherwise. - look for injuries on various parts of the body and photograph them, both long range and close up. - photographs of the place of occurrence, both long range and close up, are necessary. the injuries on the dead body should be taken in close range along with a scale. - draw sketch of the scene of occurrence indicating relative distances between the body and other physical evidence with reference to certain landmarks. - get the viscera of the deceased examined for alcohol/poison / drug.

- yellowish parchment like skin may be seen at the point of contact, which is termed as 'spark burn' in domestic electrocution (fig.25). - electrocution due to high tension wires result in charring and cleaving of the skin resulting in a large injury termed as 'crocodile skin' (fig.26). - death due to electrocution is normally accidental. however a few homicidal electrocution deaths were also reported. - the position of the electrical appliances and their conditions along with circumstantial evidences may indicate whether the death was accidental or homicidal. - burns may be seen at the point of entry in the shape of the object causing electrocution and skin may be ruptured at the point of exit. - arboreal marks (branch like) may be seen on the body in lightning cases (fig.27). - there may be linear burns on the skin due to lightning. - a few superficial burns on the skin due to metallic objects like rings, chains, bracelets, wrist watches, etc. may also be found. - clothes may be tom in zigzag manner. sometimes, clothes may be torn along the stitches. shoes if worn may burst open (fig.28). - in rare cases, the injuries on the body may be deep. fig. 25 burning due to domestic electrocution fig. 27 arboreal injury due to lightning fig. 26 burning due to high voltage - crocodile skin fig. 28 clothes torn due to lightning - check whether the wound is due to close range firing or distant firing. - if the firing is from close range find out whether it is contact fire or otherwise. in contact fire, 'muzzle stamp' or muzzle impression near the wound could be seen. - find out entry and exit wounds (fig.29). - the factors that influence size, shape, and appearance of firearm wounds are, range of firing (distance of fire), type of weapon, type of ammunition, clothing worn by the victim, and part of the body that fragmented the bullet or ricocheted the bullet (fig.30-33). range can be estimated in a laboratory. - in close range firing look for tattooing around the wound. scorching/ singeing of hair/skin also could be seen in such cases (fig.34). - if revolver/pistol is fired from a distance, the wound is typically round with neat hole along with an abrasion collar. black ring around the edges of the collar also could be seen in some cases. - in gunshot firing from close range, the entire ammunition along with wad may go inside the body forming a 'rat hole'. if the firing is from a distance, the wounds/perforations are found in a large area (fig.35). - based on the types of wounds and place of such wounds on the victim in firearm cases, it may be possible to ascertain the cause of death i.e., homicide, suicide or accident. fig. 29 arrows indicating 1 entry & 2 exit wounds
